questions.stem: An application running on a Compute Engine VM needs to read objects from a Cloud Storage bucket. What is the recommended way to grant access?

options.text: Attach a service account with the Storage Object Viewer role to the VM
options.explanation: Correct. Attaching a least-privilege service account lets the VM authenticate with rotated, short-lived credentials and no embedded keys.

options.text: Download a service account JSON key and place it on the VM
options.explanation: Wrong. Long-lived key files are a leak risk; attached service accounts avoid storing keys at all.
Wrong. Public buckets expose data to everyone — a serious misconfiguration.

options.text: Use the project Owner role on the VM's service account
options.explanation: Wrong. Owner is far too broad; it violates least privilege.

questions.stem: Which resource container sits directly above projects and lets you apply IAM policies and org policies to groups of projects?

options.explanation: Correct. Folders group projects under the organization and allow inherited IAM and org policies.
Wrong. Billing accounts handle payment, not resource hierarchy or IAM grouping.
Wrong. VPCs are networking constructs, not hierarchy containers.
Wrong. Labels are metadata key-values for organization/billing breakdowns, not IAM containers.

questions.stem: You need to run a stateless containerized web app that scales to zero when idle and requires minimal infrastructure management. Which service fits best?

options.explanation: Correct. Cloud Run runs stateless containers, scales to zero, and is fully managed — ideal for this profile.
Wrong. MIGs run VMs you manage and don't scale to zero as cleanly; more overhead than needed.
Wrong. GKE Standard means managing a cluster — heavier than required for one stateless app.

options.text: Cloud Functions (1st gen) with HTTP only
options.explanation: Wrong. Functions suit event/function units, not arbitrary containerized web apps; Cloud Run is the container-native fit.

questions.stem: A workload is fault-tolerant and can be interrupted, and you want the lowest VM cost. Which Compute Engine option fits?

options.explanation: Correct. Spot VMs offer the deepest discount for interruptible, fault-tolerant workloads.

options.text: Committed use discounts on on-demand VMs
options.explanation: Wrong. CUDs save on steady long-term usage, not short interruptible jobs.
Wrong. Sole-tenant targets compliance/licensing at premium cost.
Wrong. On-demand is the baseline price, not the cheapest for interruptible work.

questions.stem: Which service collects, queries, and alerts on logs from GCP resources?

options.explanation: Correct. Cloud Logging ingests and stores logs, supports queries, and feeds log-based metrics and alerts.
Wrong. Trace handles distributed latency tracing, not general log collection.
Wrong. Storage holds objects; it's not a logging/alerting platform (though logs can be exported there).
Wrong. Pub/Sub is messaging; it can carry log exports but doesn't query or alert on them itself.

questions.stem: Which commands/tools can deploy resources to GCP in a repeatable, scriptable way? (Select TWO)

options.text: gcloud CLI commands in a script
options.explanation: Correct. The gcloud CLI is scriptable and repeatable for provisioning and management.
Correct. Terraform declaratively provisions GCP resources and is fully repeatable.

options.text: Manually clicking through the Cloud Console
options.explanation: Wrong. Console clicks aren't repeatable or version-controlled.

options.text: Editing resources directly in the billing report
options.explanation: Wrong. Billing reports are read-only cost views; you can't deploy from them.
